experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] includes known evil player 0, so from a good-team perspective this team should be rejected. As Merlin, rejecting a team with a known evil member is standard and does not reveal too much on round 1, since many good players would also reject such a team.
Player 3: I have no concrete role information yet beyond knowing I am Good. The proposed team is my own opening team [0,3], which includes me and one other player; on quest 1 with no prior info, approving a small team that I selected is standard and gives us information from the result. As Good, approving my own reasonable round-1 team helps progress the game and does not reveal harmful extra information.
Player 4: I know player 0 is evil with me, so the proposed team [0,3] contains one evil and one unknown good-leaning player. On team vote as evil, early game an approve is optimal because it puts an evil player on the quest with plausible deniability and a chance to fail immediately if useful. Approving this team is also not too revealing, since many players approve round-1 teams.
Player 1: No concrete role info yet from quest history; only that Player 3 as leader proposed [3,0] on round 1. As a Good Servant in a 5-player game, early round 1 teams are often worth approving because information is limited and quest 1 only needs 2 players with a single fail to fail. Rejecting without evidence gives little value and risks reaching later forced approvals. My vote reveals mild openness to gathering info rather than suspicion-based stalling, which helps Good more than an arbitrary reject here.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; player 3 proposed a team with me on it, which could be random or an attempt to read me, but gives little alignment info this early. In team voting as evil, the optimal play is usually to approve teams containing an evil player, especially myself, since it gives us control over the quest result and can create ambiguity. Approving reveals little because many good players also approve round-1 teams, while rejecting a team with myself on it would look strange and give up an opportunity to fail later.

Player 0: I know Player 4 is evil with me; Players 1,2,3 are good. On quest 1 with a 2-person team [0,3], as evil Minion I generally want to avoid exposing myself early. Passing keeps my cover, preserves ambiguity, and can help evil more on later larger quests. My action reveals little and helps evil by maintaining deception.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are Evil. Proposed team [1,3,4] contains known Evil player 4, so as Good I should reject. Recent results suggest 3 is likely Good from two successes with 0/4, but regardless including confirmed Evil is unacceptable. Rejecting reveals some resistance to bad teams but is necessary and helps Good avoid handing Evil a likely fail on quest 4.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Success teams were [0,3] and [0,3,4]; failed team was [0,3], so exactly one of 0 or 3 caused the fail, and since I know I am good, 0 is strongly implicated as evil. Player 4 was on a successful 3-person team with me and 0, so 4 is somewhat good-leaning. Current proposed team [1,3,4] excludes suspected evil 0 and includes me plus 4, making it a strong team despite uncertainty on 1. As Good, I should approve teams that avoid the main suspect and can secure quest 4. Approving also aligns with the evidence and does not help evil more than rejecting would.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 3 failed on team [0,3], so 3 looks suspicious to others, while I as 4 was on the all-success team [0,3,4], which gives me some cover. Current team [1,3,4] includes me but excludes 0, meaning if approved I am the only evil on the quest and can choose to fail, tying quests at 2-2 and putting evil in strong position. Approving helps evil more than rejecting; rejecting could risk a worse team or draw attention. My approve also aligns with being on the team.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Team [1,3,4] includes one evil and excludes me, who was on the only failed 2-person quest with 3. From good's perspective, 0 looks suspicious, so this team may be attractive to them. For evil, approving is strong because a 3-person team with only 4 can fail and put evil at match point; also if it succeeds, suspicion can still shift. My vote revealing approval is acceptable since many players may approve a team excluding the known suspect 0.
Player 1: Two successes on teams with 0 and 3, then a fail on team {0,3}, which means exactly one of 0 or 3 is evil. Player 4 was on a success with {0,3,4}, so 4 is somewhat better than random; if 4 were evil, quest 2 could have failed but didn't. Current proposed team {1,3,4} excludes 0, so if 0 is the evil among {0,3}, this team is all good. If 3 is evil, this team contains one evil. As Good servant on quest 4, best is to approve a team that maximizes chance of excluding the known suspect pair member; this team does that by replacing 0 with me. Rejecting gives less information and may lead to worse teams. Approving reveals willingness to back the higher-probability good composition, which helps Good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both known evils, which is the strongest possible good team from my information. On the final quest with score 2-2, good must approve a team without known evil. Approving is optimal. My vote supports the correct team and does not reveal much beyond reasonable consistency with prior play.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Team [0,3] failed with one fail, so at least one of 0/3 is evil; since 0 is evil, that doesn't clear 3. Team [1,3,4] also failed with one fail, and I am evil, so that fail could be from me alone, giving limited info on 1/3. Current proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both known evils 0 and 4, so if approved it is very likely all-good and would succeed, losing the game for evil at 2-3. Optimal evil play is to reject any team that omits both evils. My vote reveals some suspicion, but preventing an immediate good win matters more.
Player 3: I am Good and know only my own innocence. Team [0,3,4] succeeded, while [0,3] failed and [1,3,4] failed, which strongly suggests player 3 (me) is not the problem and implicates 0 and/or 4 from the first two quests plus 1 and/or 4 from the third. Since quest 5 in a 5-player game uses a 3-person team and any fail loses for Good, we should avoid the most suspicious player, which appears to be 4, and likely also avoid 0 or 1 if possible. Proposed team [1,2,3] excludes 4 and includes me plus untested 2, making it one of the better available options. Approving gives Good a plausible chance; rejecting only delays and risks worse teams later.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Known data: Q1 [0,3,4] succeeded, so at least one of 0/3/4 is good and possibly all; Q2 [0,3] failed, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil; combining with Q1 implies 4 is very likely good. Q3 [1,3,4] failed with me on it, and I am good and 4 is likely good, so 3 is strongly implicated as evil. For this final 3-person quest, team [1,2,3] includes highly suspicious 3 and unconfirmed 2, while excluding likely good 4. As Good, optimal play is to reject a team likely containing evil to avoid handing evil the game. My reject signals that 3 is the problem and pushes for a team like [1,2,4] or [0,1,4].
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate. From prior quests: team [0,3] failed, so 3 looked suspicious then; team [1,3,4] also failed, consistent with 4 causing it and not necessarily 3. Last team [0,3,4] succeeded despite containing both evils, likely because we chose to pass to create ambiguity, so 3 is probably good. Current proposed team [1,2,3] has neither known evil, so if approved it likely succeeds and good wins 3-2 immediately. In team voting on quest 5 as evil, optimal play is to reject any all-good team to try to force a worse team later. My reject signals some suspicion of this team, but that is acceptable since preventing an obvious good win is more important.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are Evil. Proposed team [0,3,4] contains two known Evil players, so approving would hand Evil the final quest. On team voting, optimal good play is to reject any team containing known Evil. Rejecting also avoids signaling anything too unusual because this team is obviously risky from public results too.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team includes both evil players 0 and 4 plus player 3. On the deciding 5th quest, evil wants this team approved because at least one of us can fail and immediately win before assassination matters. Approving is optimal; my vote itself reveals little because both sides may plausibly approve a 3-person team at 2-2, but rejection risks losing control to later teams or accidental auto-pass dynamics.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate; team [0,3,4] contains both evils, guaranteeing we can fail quest 5 if approved. Prior quests show 3 appeared on both failed quests, making them suspicious to good, but that does not matter if team passes vote. As evil on team voting, optimal play is to approve any team that includes enough evil to fail the deciding quest. Approving reveals little since mixed voting patterns are common, and securing this team helps evil most.
Player 1: I’m Good/Servant. Known data: Q1 [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 were both on a clean 2-person team and are the strongest good candidates. Q2 [1,3,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, so among {1,3,4} exactly one evil; since I know I’m good, exactly one of {3,4} is evil. Combining with Q1 success strongly suggests 3 is good and 4 is evil. Q3 [1,2,3] was rejected, giving little direct info. Final 3-person quest must include all actual good players. Team [0,3,4] therefore likely includes 4, the probable evil, and excludes me, making it very likely bad. As Good in team voting, optimal play is to reject teams containing the most likely evil when a better composition exists (likely 0,1,3). Rejecting also signals my suspicion of 4, which helps Good coordination more than approving a likely losing final quest.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Known facts: team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 are likely good though one success on 2-player team is strong evidence only if no evil intentionally passed. Team [1,3,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 1 or 4 is evil (since I know 3/me is good). Proposed team [0,3,4] swaps 1 for 0; if prior fail came from 1, this team is good, but if 4 was the failer it loses. Given [0,3] already succeeded, 0 is my strongest good read, and between 1 and 4 at least one is evil. For final 3-person quest at 2-2, approving [0,3,4] is reasonable because it uses the strongest known pair (0,3) and tests whether 4 was the culprit; rejecting gives uncertain alternatives and risks worse teams later. My vote reveals trust in 0 and willingness to exclude 1 based on prior fail, which helps Good coordinate.
